era_context: The Han Dynasty is in its final year of nominal authority; the Three Kingdoms period is crystallizing. Cao Cao dominates northern China; Liu Bei and Sun Quan control southwestern and southern territories respectively. In the Roman Empire, Elagabalus is in his second year as emperor, facing military and political opposition. The Sassanid Empire is consolidating power in Persia. This is a year of transition and consolidation across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe (sparse archaeological record for this specific year)
Mesoamerica (Maya records exist but are sparse for 219 CE)

events.title: Guan Yu's military campaign and death; loss of Jingzhou to Sun Quan
events.region: Central China (Jingzhou region, Yangtze River valley)
events.description: Guan Yu, one of Liu Bei's most celebrated generals, launches a military campaign to expand Liu Bei's territory northward from Jingzhou. However, Sun Quan's forces, under the command of Lü Meng, attack from the south and capture Jingzhou while Guan Yu is engaged in the north. Guan Yu is defeated and executed by Sun Quan's forces. This loss is catastrophic for Liu Bei's strategic position and marks a major shift in the balance of power between Shu and Wu. Guan Yu's death becomes a legendary moment in Chinese history and literature, immortalized in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Romance of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguo Yanyi), attributed to Luo Guanzhong (14th century CE)

events.certainty_note: Guan Yu's death in 219 CE is well-attested in Chinese sources. The exact circumstances are detailed in Sanguozhi and later compilations. The date is secure.
cross_references: Follows from 220-002 (Liu Bei's consolidation of Yizhou)
Leads to 221-002 (Liu Bei's response and proclamation as emperor)

events.title: Cao Cao's campaign against Ma Chao and Han Sui in the northwest
events.description: Cao Cao launches a major military campaign against the warlords Ma Chao and Han Sui in the Guanzhong region (modern Shaanxi). After initial setbacks, Cao Cao defeats the coalition and consolidates control over northwestern China. This campaign extends Cao Cao's territorial control and eliminates a significant rival. The campaign demonstrates Cao Cao's military superiority and his ability to project power across vast distances. The victory solidifies Cao Cao's position as the dominant northern warlord.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Chronicles of the Three Kingdoms), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)

events.certainty_note: Cao Cao's campaign against Ma Chao and Han Sui is dated to 219 CE in most sources, though some sources place it in 217–218 CE. The dating is secure to within 1–2 years.
cross_references: Contemporary with 219-001 (Guan Yu's campaign and death)
Leads to 220-001 (Cao Cao's consolidation as King of Wei)

events.title: Elagabalus attempts religious reforms; conflict with Roman traditionalists
events.description: Elagabalus continues his attempts to elevate the cult of the Syrian sun god Elagabal above traditional Roman religion, including Jupiter. He attempts to move the sacred stone of the god to a new temple and promote Elagabal worship among the Roman elite. These religious innovations provoke strong opposition from traditionalist senators and the military, who view them as sacrilege and a threat to Roman identity. The conflict between Elagabalus and the Roman establishment intensifies, contributing to the instability of his reign.

sources.name: Historia Augusta (Augustan History), compiled in 4th century CE

events.certainty_note: Elagabalus's religious reforms are attested in later sources, but the Historia Augusta is notoriously unreliable. The exact year of specific reforms is uncertain; 219 CE is approximate.
cross_references: Leads to 222-002 (Elagabalus assassinated; religious reforms reversed)

events.title: Sassanid consolidation continues; Ardashir I defeats remaining Parthian forces
events.description: Ardashir I continues his military campaigns against the declining Parthian Arsacid Empire, defeating remaining Parthian forces and consolidating Sassanid control over Mesopotamia and Persia. By 219 CE, Ardashir has effectively eliminated Parthian power in most regions, though the final defeat of Artabanus V is traditionally dated to 224 CE. The Sassanid Empire emerges as the dominant power in the Near East, replacing the Parthian Empire that had ruled for nearly five centuries.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Ardashir's campaigns is uncertain. The year 219 CE is approximate; the final defeat of Artabanus V is traditionally dated to 224 CE.
cross_references: Leads to 224-001 (Final defeat of Artabanus V)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Guan Yu's death is traditionally given as 219 CE, but some modern scholars argue for 220 CE based on alternative source interpretations. The exact circumstances of his capture and execution are embellished in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms; the Sanguozhi provides a more restrained account. Elagabalus's religious reforms are often exaggerated in the Historia Augusta; modern scholarship suggests they were less radical than portrayed. The Sassanid conquest of Parthia is a gradual process; the year 219 CE represents one phase rather than a decisive moment.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 219 CE is dominated by Chinese sources (Sanguozhi and later compilations), which are detailed but compiled centuries after the events. Roman sources are similarly late and often unreliable. The Three Kingdoms period is well-documented in Chinese historiography, but the exact dating of military campaigns is sometimes uncertain. South Asian, African, and Mesoamerican sources for this year are sparse or absent. The loss of Jingzhou is a pivotal moment in Chinese history, extensively documented in later literature but less so in contemporary sources.

graph_edges.note: Guan Yu's death and loss of Jingzhou prompted Liu Bei's response and proclamation as emperor
Cao Cao's military victories consolidated his position as dominant northern warlord, leading to his kingship
Guan Yu's campaign and Cao Cao's northwestern campaign occurred simultaneously, defining the military balance
Elagabalus's religious reforms and conflicts with traditionalists contributed to his assassination
